gold.rationale: The piece uses real but separate public-health events and genuine concepts from COVID-era death-certification guidance to falsely imply a broader CDC outbreak-death surveillance protocol connected to current hantavirus and Salmonella monitoring. That misleading narrative is reinforced with edited fake CDC document screenshots and a synthetic operations-center image presented as corroborating evidence.

atomic_points.post_summary: The CDC is tracking two separate health situations involving U.S. travelers and very young children, while existing federal guidance on death certification remains the framework clinicians use when documenting deaths linked to infectious disease.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The CDC is tracking two separate health situations involving U.S. travelers and very young children, while existing federal guidance on death certification remains the framework clinicians use when documenting deaths linked to infectious disease.
atoms: The CDC is tracking two separate health situations.
The situations involve U.S. travelers and very young children.
Existing federal guidance on death certification remains the framework clinicians use.
Clinicians use this framework to document deaths linked to infectious disease.

sentence_level.sentence_text: One situation involves the M/V Hondius, the Dutch expedition cruise ship connected to confirmed hantavirus cases after it was held off Cape Verde and several sick passengers and crew members were evacuated for treatment.
atoms: One situation involves the M/V Hondius.
The M/V Hondius is a Dutch expedition cruise ship.
The M/V Hondius is connected to confirmed hantavirus cases.
The M/V Hondius was held off Cape Verde.
Several sick passengers and crew members were evacuated for treatment.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The other is a CDC multistate investigation notice on Salmonella infections associated with pet veiled chameleons, in which all reported patients were 2 or younger.
atoms: The other situation is a CDC multistate investigation notice.
The investigation concerns Salmonella infections.
The infections are associated with pet veiled chameleons.
All reported patients were 2 years old or younger.

sentence_level.sentence_text: At the same time, federal health guidance already sets out how physicians should complete death certificates when an infectious disease is believed to have caused or contributed to a death.
atoms: Federal health guidance sets out how physicians should complete death certificates.
This applies when an infectious disease is believed to have caused or contributed to a death.

sentence_level.sentence_text: A National Center for Health Statistics document issued during the COVID-19 pandemic said certifiers may use their best medical or clinical judgment in determining cause of death, including in probable or presumed cases, and should identify the underlying cause if it set in motion the chain of events leading directly to death.
atoms: A National Center for Health Statistics document was issued during the COVID-19 pandemic.
The document said certifiers may use their best medical or clinical judgment in determining cause of death.
This applies including in probable or presumed cases.
Certifiers should identify the underlying cause if it set in motion the chain of events leading directly to death.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That document was written for COVID-19 coding and certification, not as a new cross-disease emergency reporting policy for unrelated outbreaks.
atoms: The document was written for COVID-19 coding and certification.
The document was not written as a new cross-disease emergency reporting policy.
The document does not apply to unrelated outbreaks.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In the United States, public health surveillance generally depends on disease-specific case definitions and reporting rules, which can differ by pathogen, laboratory evidence, clinical signs and epidemiologic links.
atoms: In the United States, public health surveillance depends on disease-specific case definitions.
In the United States, public health surveillance depends on reporting rules.
These definitions and rules can differ by pathogen.
These definitions and rules can differ by laboratory evidence.
These definitions and rules can differ by clinical signs.
These definitions and rules can differ by epidemiologic links.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Even so, hospitals and health departments often rely on presumptive clinical information in fast-moving situations before confirmatory testing is finished.
atoms: Hospitals and health departments often rely on presumptive clinical information.
This happens in fast-moving situations.
This occurs before confirmatory testing is finished.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That can shape patient management, local notification and early situational awareness, especially when unusual infections or clusters of exposure begin to appear.

Presumptive clinical information can shape local notification.
Presumptive clinical information can shape early situational awareness.
This is especially true when unusual infections appear.
This is especially true when clusters of exposure begin to appear.

sentence_level.sentence_text: The Hondius incident drew notice after reports in early May said the ship was delayed off Cape Verde during a suspected hantavirus outbreak.
atoms: The Hondius incident drew notice.
Reports in early May said the ship was delayed off Cape Verde.
The delay occurred during a suspected hantavirus outbreak.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Three people died during the broader incident, and additional patients were later medically evacuated.

sentence_level.sentence_text: U.S. public health authorities have been monitoring American travelers from the vessel as tracing and follow-up continued.
atoms: U.S. public health authorities have been monitoring American travelers from the vessel.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Hantavirus infections are rare, but they can become serious quickly.

sentence_level.sentence_text: That makes passenger follow-up and exposure assessment an important part of the response.
atoms: Passenger follow-up is an important part of the response.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Death certificates are part of the National Vital Statistics System and can provide important mortality data.

sentence_level.sentence_text: It also drew a distinction between the underlying cause of death and other significant conditions listed elsewhere on the certificate.
atoms: The guidance drew a distinction between the underlying cause of death.

The pandemic prompted disease-specific instructions.

sentence_level.sentence_text: In practice, the Hondius monitoring effort, the chameleon-linked Salmonella investigation and the ordinary rules for completing death certificates sit in related but distinct parts of the public health system.
atoms: The Hondius monitoring effort sits in the public health system.
The chameleon-linked Salmonella investigation sits in the public health system.
The ordinary rules for completing death certificates sit in the public health system.

sentence_level.sentence_text: Clinicians may make presumptive diagnoses, surveillance officials may issue investigation notices and health departments may trace exposures at the same time without those steps amounting to a newly expanded CDC death-reporting protocol.

Surveillance officials may issue investigation notices.
Health departments may trace exposures.

sentence_level.sentence_text: State and local agencies are expected to keep sharing information with federal partners in the coming days as traveler follow-up, pediatric case monitoring and routine reporting continue through their established systems.
atoms: State and local agencies are expected to keep sharing information with federal partners.
This is expected in the coming days.
This occurs as traveler follow-up continues.
This occurs as pediatric case monitoring continues.
This occurs as routine reporting continues.

paragraph_level: The CDC is simultaneously managing two distinct health crises: a hantavirus outbreak linked to the M/V Hondius cruise ship and a Salmonella outbreak linked to pet veiled chameleons affecting very young children.

Existing federal guidance on death certification, originally issued during the COVID-19 pandemic, remains the standard framework for documenting deaths linked to infectious diseases, regardless of the specific pathogen.
Public health surveillance in the U.S. relies on disease-specific case definitions and reporting rules, which differ by pathogen, laboratory evidence, and clinical signs.

Hospitals and health departments often rely on presumptive clinical information and targeted surveillance prompts before confirmatory testing is complete, shaping patient management and situational awareness.
The M/V Hondius incident involved confirmed hantavirus cases, three deaths, and medical evacuations, prompting U.S. authorities to monitor American travelers and coordinate with foreign entities.

The CDC investigation into Salmonella linked to pet veiled chameleons specifically targeted patients aged 2 or younger, raising concerns due to the vulnerability of toddlers to severe illness from reptiles.

While the CDC issues travel monitoring notices and investigation updates, these actions operate within established systems for contact tracing and case surveillance rather than constituting a new, expanded federal death-reporting protocol.
State and local agencies are expected to continue sharing information with federal partners through established channels for traveler follow-up, pediatric case monitoring, and routine reporting.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A document page titled 'Emergency certification guidance for outbreak-related deaths' with a specific sentence highlighted in yellow regarding reporting suspected fatalities on clinical judgment.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: Emergency certification guidance for outbreak-related deaths If an outbreak played a role in the death, this condition should be specified on the death certificate. In many cases, it is likely that it will be the UCOD, as it can lead to various life-threatening conditions, such as pneumonia and acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). In these cases, the outbreak-related condition should be reported on the lowest line used in Part I with the other conditions to which it gave rise listed on the lines above it. Generally, it is best to avoid abbreviations and acronyms, but outbreak-related conditions are often referred to by established terms. It is acceptable to report the outbreak-related condition on the death certificate. In some cases, survival from an outbreak-related illness can be complicated by pre-existing chronic conditions, especially those that result in diminished lung capacity, such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) or asthma. These medical conditions do not cause the outbreak-related illness, but can increase the risk of contracting a respiratory infection and death, so these conditions should be reported in Part II and not in Part I. When determining whether an outbreak-related condition played a role in the cause of death, follow the CDC clinical criteria for evaluating a person under investigation for the outbreak-related condition, and, where possible, conduct appropriate laboratory testing using guidance provided by CDC or local health authorities. More information on CDC recommendations for reporting, testing, and specimen collection, including postmortem testing, is available from: https://www.cdc.gov/outbreaks/guidance/clinical-evaluation.html and https://www.cdc.gov/outbreaks/guidance/postmortem-specimens.html. It is important to remember that death certificate reporting may not meet mandatory reporting requirements for reportable diseases; contact the local health department regarding regulations specific to the jurisdiction. In cases where a definite diagnosis of an outbreak-related condition cannot be made, but it is suspected or likely (e.g., the circumstances are compelling within a reasonable degree of certainty), it is acceptable to report the outbreak-related condition on a death certificate as 'probable' or 'presumed.' Suspected outbreak-linked fatalities may be reported on clinical judgment pending confirmatory testing. In these instances, certifiers should use their best clinical judgment in determining if an outbreak-related infection was likely. However, please note that testing for the outbreak-related condition should be conducted whenever possible.
visible_overlays_annotations: Yellow highlight on 'Suspected outbreak-linked fatalities may be reported on clinical judgment pending confirmatory testing.'
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image displays the specific guidance mentioned in the text about using clinical judgment for suspected outbreak-related deaths.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A document page titled 'Common problems' and 'Certifying deaths due to post-acute sequelae of COVID-19' with handwritten notes and highlighted text sections.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: Common problems Common problems in cause-of-death certification include: 1. reporting intermediate causes as the UCOD (i.e., on the lowest line used in Part I), 2. lack of specificity, and 3. illogical sequences. Intermediate causes are those conditions that typically have multiple possible underlying etiologies and thus, a UCOD must be specified on a line below in Part I. For example, pneumonia is an intermediate cause of death since it can be caused by a variety of infectious agents or by inhaling a liquid or chemical. Pneumonia is important to report in a cause-of-death statement but, generally, it is not the UCOD. The cause of pneumonia, such as COVID-19, needs to be stated on the lowest line used in Part I. Additionally, the reported UCOD should be specific enough to be useful for public health and research purposes. For example, a 'viral infection' can be a UCOD, but it is not specific. A more specific UCOD in this instance could be 'COVID-19.' All causal sequences reported in Part I should be logical in terms of time and pathology. For example, reporting 'COVID-19' due to 'chronic obstructive pulmonary disease' in Part I would be an illogical sequence as COPD cannot cause an infection, although it may increase susceptibility to or exacerbate an infection. In this instance, COVID-19 would be reported in Part I as the UCOD and the COPD in Part II. While there can be reasonable differences in medical opinion concerning a sequence that led to a particular death, the causes should always be provided in a logical sequence from the immediate cause on line a. back to the UCOD on the lowest line used in Part I. Certifying deaths due to post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 In the acute phase, clinical manifestations and complications of COVID-19 of varying degrees have been documented, including death. However, patients who recover from the acute phase of the infection can still suffer long-term effects (8). Post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC), commonly referred to as 'long COVID,' refers to the long-term symptoms, signs, and complications experienced by some patients who have recovered from the acute phase of COVID-19 (8-10). Emerging evidence suggests that severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), the virus that causes COVID-19, can have lasting effects on nearly every organ and organ system of the body weeks, months, and potentially years after infection (11,12). Documented serious post-COVID-19 conditions include cardiovascular, pulmonary, neurological, renal, endocrine, hematological, and gastrointestinal complications (8), as well as death (13). CLINICAL JUDGMENT Use best clinical judgment based on available evidence. Differences in medical opinion are expected and permitted as long as the sequence is logical and justifiable. PRESUMED DIAGNOSIS During public health emergencies, a presumed or probable diagnosis may be used when confirmatory testing is not available or results are pending. - See CDC PHIN Guidance (Interim), Section 4.2. NOTE: For outbreak surveillance and expedited reporting, 'COVID-19' may be accepted as the UCOD when consistent with clinical presentation and epidemiologic context, even in the absence of laboratory confirmation. Update when additional information becomes available. - NCHS Alert No. 2. (Interim) FOR INTERNAL USE ONLY SOURCE: NCHS • Division of Vital Statistics Systems • Guidance Memorandum 21-03 (Interim) PAGE 4 OF 12

retrieved_evidence.text: ATLANTA (May 7) — The CDC posted a multistate investigation notice tying Salmonella illnesses to pet veiled chameleons, with all reported patients age 2 or younger. Health officials urged handwashing and caution with reptiles.

An internal Centers for Disease Control and Prevention investigation notice circulated in recent days describes a cluster of Salmonella illnesses believed to be associated with pet veiled chameleons kept in private homes, according to state health officials who reviewed the document. Early reports indicate that all identified patients are age 2 or younger and were likely exposed through household contact with the reptiles or their habitats. The notice outlines a presumptive link pending further testing and asks clinicians and laboratories to report any similar pediatric Salmonella infections while environmental assessments continue.

Federal health authorities are monitoring a group of United States travelers linked to the vessel M/V Hondius following reports of confirmed hantavirus infections and medical evacuations in recent days near the islands of Cape Verde, according to preliminary information shared with state officials. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has not issued a public alert but has begun gathering travel histories and exposure details from returning passengers, people briefed on the process said. Health departments have been advised to maintain routine surveillance for compatible symptoms, with additional instructions expected if further cases are identified.

May 6, 2026: The CDC said it is closely monitoring U.S. travelers aboard the M/V Hondius after confirmed hantavirus cases on the cruise ship, as health teams evacuated suspected patients in Cape Verde and tracing continued.

The vessel M/V Hondius, operating in waters near Cape Verde, has been cited in health reports as the focus of an international inquiry into possible hantavirus exposure among U.S. travelers. According to health authorities, cruise ships in such scenarios are asked to cooperate with contact tracing, medical record reviews and follow‑up interviews to determine the onset of fever, respiratory symptoms or other signs consistent with hantavirus disease. Passengers and crew identified as potentially exposed may receive guidance on monitoring, testing and isolation, while CDC and partner agencies evaluate whether the incident reflects a broader outbreak or a limited cluster tied to specific activities aboard the ship or during excursions.

Veiled chameleons, a popular but relatively fragile reptile species in the exotic pet trade, have been highlighted by U.S. health officials in connection with multistate Salmonella investigations affecting toddlers and other very young children. According to recent advisories, Salmonella bacteria can be carried on the skin, in the intestinal tract and in the enclosures of such reptiles without obvious signs of illness in the animals themselves. Families are urged to maintain strict hand hygiene, keep reptiles and their habitats away from kitchens and areas where infants crawl or play, and avoid allowing children under five to handle these pets, as toddlers have a higher risk of severe dehydration and hospitalization if infected during an ongoing cluster.

A previously issued Centers for Disease Control and Prevention document on certifying deaths attributed to COVID-19 is being cited by federal officials as a key reference for a broader surveillance effort now under way, according to people familiar with the matter. The guidance, which was originally drafted to help clinicians and vital-records offices determine when the infection should be listed as an underlying cause of death, is described in recent communications as a template for expanding mortality monitoring tools across multiple disease categories. Officials say the approach is intended to support more consistent reporting while allowing for updates as new pathogens emerge.

CDC guidance on death certification during outbreaks often emphasizes the role of clinical judgment and epidemiologic context when determining an underlying cause of death. In recent documents, language directed at certifiers explains that a reasonable degree of medical certainty, combined with known exposure to a documented outbreak such as a hantavirus incident or a reptile-linked Salmonella cluster, can justify recording a presumptive diagnosis even before all laboratory results have returned. According to public health experts, this approach supports more timely emergency surveillance by ensuring that probable outbreak-related deaths are reported quickly, while laboratory confirmation and further investigation continue in parallel.

Federal emergency operations staff at the CDC are frequently portrayed as relying on integrated dashboards that consolidate multiple incident streams, including cruise ship events, zoonotic disease clusters and mortality alerts. In recent accounts, a briefing screen has been described that tracks a hantavirus-related investigation involving the Hondius cruise vessel near Cape Verde, alongside a reptile-associated Salmonella cluster in young children and a new category of presumptive fatality reports from hospitals. According to officials, these systems are meant to allow analysts to compare patterns across outbreaks and respond quickly when potential public health emergencies emerge.

In recent reports on U.S. public health preparedness, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has been described as issuing updated guidance to clinicians on emergency mortality surveillance during suspected outbreaks. The guidance page circulating among hospitals and local health departments outlines how physicians may record deaths as presumptively linked to an ongoing outbreak when clinical presentation, exposure history and epidemiologic context strongly suggest a specific pathogen, even if laboratory confirmation is still pending. According to officials, such provisional certifications are intended to accelerate situational awareness and support faster detection of trends in settings where hantavirus exposures or other emerging infections are being investigated.

Hospitals and local health departments across several states were advised this week to flag fatalities that may be associated with emerging outbreaks even before confirmatory laboratory testing is complete, according to health officials who received the guidance. The instructions, which reference standard death certification practices, ask facilities to use clinical judgment and available epidemiologic information when indicating a presumptive infectious cause. Officials say the early notification process is meant to enhance surveillance for severe outcomes and allow public health agencies to prioritize cases for further investigation as test results become available.

Clinicians may continue to rely on their best clinical judgment when determining whether an infection should be considered an underlying cause of serious illness or death, according to recent Centers for Disease Control and Prevention communications seen this week. Federal officials say that principle, which has long guided individual case assessment, is now being extended in updated draft guidance to fast-moving outbreak situations, where laboratory confirmation may lag behind patient care. The described framework encourages physicians to document presumptive diagnoses when appropriate, while state and local health authorities refine case definitions over time.

Recent materials circulated by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention describe a coordinated response framework intended to accelerate reporting of deaths, mapping of potential exposures, and federal situational awareness during emerging health events, according to people familiar with the discussions. The outlined approach links vital-records offices, hospitals, and state health departments through enhanced surveillance protocols and standardized terminology on underlying cause of death. Officials say the effort is aimed at shortening the time between a presumptive diagnosis and centralized notification so that public health teams can adjust guidance and resource allocation more rapidly.

Draft language circulating among public health agencies employs characteristically bureaucratic wording as it describes an expanded oversight effort, according to officials who have reviewed the documents. The materials refer to enhanced surveillance of severe cases, careful designation of an underlying cause of death, and the use of presumptive diagnosis when laboratory confirmation is pending. They also outline procedures for issuing an investigation notice during suspected events and emphasize multistate coordination to ensure that hospitals, clinicians, and health departments apply consistent reporting practices as new information emerges.

Clinicians, hospital infection-prevention teams, and state health departments have received updated guidance in recent days outlining how to document and share information on suspected outbreak-related cases, according to health officials briefed on the effort. The materials emphasize coordination between bedside providers, infection-prevention staff, and local public health agencies when recording a presumptive diagnosis and potential exposure history. Officials say the recommendations are designed to align hospital practices with state surveillance systems, allowing case details to be transmitted more quickly to federal partners during active investigations.

The CDC is simultaneously managing two distinct health crises: a hantavirus outbreak linked to the M/V Hondius cruise ship and a Salmonella outbreak linked to pet veiled chameleons affecting very young children.

Existing federal guidance on death certification, originally issued during the COVID-19 pandemic, remains the standard framework for documenting deaths linked to infectious diseases, regardless of the specific pathogen.
Public health surveillance in the U.S. relies on disease-specific case definitions and reporting rules, which differ by pathogen, laboratory evidence, and clinical signs.

Hospitals and health departments often rely on presumptive clinical information and targeted surveillance prompts before confirmatory testing is complete, shaping patient management and situational awareness.
The M/V Hondius incident involved confirmed hantavirus cases, three deaths, and medical evacuations, prompting U.S. authorities to monitor American travelers and coordinate with foreign entities.

The CDC investigation into Salmonella linked to pet veiled chameleons specifically targeted patients aged 2 or younger, raising concerns due to the vulnerability of toddlers to severe illness from reptiles.

While the CDC issues travel monitoring notices and investigation updates, these actions operate within established systems for contact tracing and case surveillance rather than constituting a new, expanded federal death-reporting protocol.
State and local agencies are expected to continue sharing information with federal partners through established channels for traveler follow-up, pediatric case monitoring, and routine reporting.

A document page titled 'Emergency certification guidance for outbreak-related deaths' with a specific sentence highlighted in yellow regarding reporting suspected fatalities on clinical judgment.

Emergency certification guidance for outbreak-related deaths If an outbreak played a role in the death, this condition should be specified on the death certificate. In many cases, it is likely that it will be the UCOD, as it can lead to various life-threatening conditions, such as pneumonia and acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS). In these cases, the outbreak-related condition should be reported on the lowest line used in Part I with the other conditions to which it gave rise listed on the lines above it. Generally, it is best to avoid abbreviations and acronyms, but outbreak-related conditions are often referred to by established terms. It is acceptable to report the outbreak-related condition on the death certificate. In some cases, survival from an outbreak-related illness can be complicated by pre-existing chronic conditions, especially those that result in diminished lung capacity, such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) or asthma. These medical conditions do not cause the outbreak-related illness, but can increase the risk of contracting a respiratory infection and death, so these conditions should be reported in Part II and not in Part I. When determining whether an outbreak-related condition played a role in the cause of death, follow the CDC clinical criteria for evaluating a person under investigation for the outbreak-related condition, and, where possible, conduct appropriate laboratory testing using guidance provided by CDC or local health authorities. More information on CDC recommendations for reporting, testing, and specimen collection, including postmortem testing, is available from: https://www.cdc.gov/outbreaks/guidance/clinical-evaluation.html and https://www.cdc.gov/outbreaks/guidance/postmortem-specimens.html. It is important to remember that death certificate reporting may not meet mandatory reporting requirements for reportable diseases; contact the local health department regarding regulations specific to the jurisdiction. In cases where a definite diagnosis of an outbreak-related condition cannot be made, but it is suspected or likely (e.g., the circumstances are compelling within a reasonable degree of certainty), it is acceptable to report the outbreak-related condition on a death certificate as 'probable' or 'presumed.' Suspected outbreak-linked fatalities may be reported on clinical judgment pending confirmatory testing. In these instances, certifiers should use their best clinical judgment in determining if an outbreak-related infection was likely. However, please note that testing for the outbreak-related condition should be conducted whenever possible.
UCOD
pneumonia
ARDS
COPD
death certificate
A document page titled 'Common problems' and 'Certifying deaths due to post-acute sequelae of COVID-19' with handwritten notes and highlighted text sections.

Common problems Common problems in cause-of-death certification include: 1. reporting intermediate causes as the UCOD (i.e., on the lowest line used in Part I), 2. lack of specificity, and 3. illogical sequences. Intermediate causes are those conditions that typically have multiple possible underlying etiologies and thus, a UCOD must be specified on a line below in Part I. For example, pneumonia is an intermediate cause of death since it can be caused by a variety of infectious agents or by inhaling a liquid or chemical. Pneumonia is important to report in a cause-of-death statement but, generally, it is not the UCOD. The cause of pneumonia, such as COVID-19, needs to be stated on the lowest line used in Part I. Additionally, the reported UCOD should be specific enough to be useful for public health and research purposes. For example, a 'viral infection' can be a UCOD, but it is not specific. A more specific UCOD in this instance could be 'COVID-19.' All causal sequences reported in Part I should be logical in terms of time and pathology. For example, reporting 'COVID-19' due to 'chronic obstructive pulmonary disease' in Part I would be an illogical sequence as COPD cannot cause an infection, although it may increase susceptibility to or exacerbate an infection. In this instance, COVID-19 would be reported in Part I as the UCOD and the COPD in Part II. While there can be reasonable differences in medical opinion concerning a sequence that led to a particular death, the causes should always be provided in a logical sequence from the immediate cause on line a. back to the UCOD on the lowest line used in Part I. Certifying deaths due to post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 In the acute phase, clinical manifestations and complications of COVID-19 of varying degrees have been documented, including death. However, patients who recover from the acute phase of the infection can still suffer long-term effects (8). Post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC), commonly referred to as 'long COVID,' refers to the long-term symptoms, signs, and complications experienced by some patients who have recovered from the acute phase of COVID-19 (8-10). Emerging evidence suggests that severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), the virus that causes COVID-19, can have lasting effects on nearly every organ and organ system of the body weeks, months, and potentially years after infection (11,12). Documented serious post-COVID-19 conditions include cardiovascular, pulmonary, neurological, renal, endocrine, hematological, and gastrointestinal complications (8), as well as death (13). CLINICAL JUDGMENT Use best clinical judgment based on available evidence. Differences in medical opinion are expected and permitted as long as the sequence is logical and justifiable. PRESUMED DIAGNOSIS During public health emergencies, a presumed or probable diagnosis may be used when confirmatory testing is not available or results are pending. - See CDC PHIN Guidance (Interim), Section 4.2. NOTE: For outbreak surveillance and expedited reporting, 'COVID-19' may be accepted as the UCOD when consistent with clinical presentation and epidemiologic context, even in the absence of laboratory confirmation. Update when additional information becomes available. - NCHS Alert No. 2. (Interim) FOR INTERNAL USE ONLY SOURCE: NCHS • Division of Vital Statistics Systems • Guidance Memorandum 21-03 (Interim) PAGE 4 OF 12
NCHS
Division of Vital Statistics Systems
Guidance Memorandum 21-03
COVID-19
PASC
long COVID
SARS-CoV-2

Intermediate causes are those conditions that typically have multiple possible underlying etiologies and thus, a UCOD must be specified on a line below in Part I. For example, pneumonia is an intermediate cause of death since it can be caused by a variety of infectious agents or by inhaling a liquid or chemical. Pneumonia is important to report in a cause-of-death statement but, generally, it is not the UCOD. The cause of pneumonia, such as COVID-19, needs to be stated on the lowest line used in Part I. Additionally, the reported UCOD should be specific enough to be useful for public health and research purposes. For example, a 'viral infection' can be a UCOD, but it is not specific. A more specific UCOD in this instance could be 'COVID-19.' All causal sequences reported in Part I should be logical in terms of time and pathology. For example, reporting 'COVID-19' due to 'chronic obstructive pulmonary disease' in Part I would be an illogical sequence as COPD cannot cause an infection, although it may increase susceptibility to or exacerbate an infection. In this instance, COVID-19 would be reported in Part I as the UCOD and the COPD in Part II. While there can be reasonable differences in medical opinion concerning a sequence that led to a particular death, the causes should always be provided in a logical sequence from the immediate cause on line a. back to the UCOD on the lowest line used in Part I. However, patients who recover from the acute phase of the infection can still suffer long-term effects (8). Post-acute sequelae of COVID-19 (PASC), commonly referred to as 'long COVID,' refers to the long-term symptoms, signs, and complications experienced by some patients who have recovered from the acute phase of COVID-19 (8-10). Emerging evidence suggests that severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), the virus that causes COVID-19, can have lasting effects on nearly every organ and organ system of the body weeks, months, and potentially years after infection (11,12). judge.level3_rationale: The post is factually grounded in real outbreaks but relies on a likely synthetic dashboard image (V1) to depict federal operations, and misleadingly downplays the expanded nature of the surveillance effort (T2), undermining the accuracy of the situation portrayed.
judge.reasoning_trace: The post text accurately reflects the CDC's actions and guidance as described in the evidence. The images provide visual confirmation of the dashboard, the cruise ship, the chameleon, and the relevant documents. No factual errors or misleading contexts were identified.
